sentences.text: Well, we just stayed there.
And we-- we-- it was all right there for a while.
Another time, the same thing happened.
There was another search.
And the Jewish boyfriend of this lady was staying there for the night.
And he chased up the stairs and wanted to go in-- well, he went in, and he wanted to close the trap door.
He was so nervous.
And he wanted to-- he nearly left us out.
That was terrible, and we-- we had practically no room.
You know, I mean, I had one foot in the toilet because there was no room for three people.
But luckily, we-- we just managed to-- to get in there.
And-- and-- and stay.
But we heard for instance-- another instance where something like that happened.
And there was a hiding place as well.
But the Germans felt the beds of-- and they were warm still.
So they realized there must have been people there little while ago.
And they really pulled the house completely apart and found the people, of course.
And they were all caught.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sometimes we listened-- we were not allowed, of course, to listen to foreign radio.
But occasionally we-- Mrs.
Klompe, and especially when the boyfriend was there as well, we wanted to listen to the BBC.
The BBC sent out to all the occupied countries news in their language, kind of, because we only knew the German news, and that was of course always bad for us and good for the Germans.
But of course it wasn't always like that.
So they wanted to give hope to the people, if a battle was won or if something good happened.

So we did listen, and they did tell-- because I think this is important-- that they did know that there were concentration camps in the east, even they mentioned Buchenwald and Auschwitz, where there were gas chambers and where people were gassed and cremated.
So we did know this information.
And they kept giving us hope.
They said, if you are in hiding, be careful.
Stay in hiding.
It won't be so long anymore.
We'll try our best to finish the war as soon as possible.
And you know-- so we had hope.
And of course, when the Africa war started and the Germans were starting to lose, it was very hopeful for us.
Because we went in hiding in June '42.
But we thought, by Christmas it will be the end.
Nobody really thought a minute that it would last that long.
If we would have known we would have to stay in hiding for years, you know, I don't think we might have-- perhaps not have attempted it.
Because it was really very difficult.
And as well, my father had jewelry, and-- because the-- with us, I don't think our-- Mrs.
Klompe, I don't think she asked a lot of money.
But of course, life is expensive.
And with food being rationed and everything, it-- it was still quite a bit to pay.
But the woman where my father and brother was in hiding, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was a single woman.
And she started to blackmail them.
She wanted more and more money from my father.
And my father said, well-- he did give it, of course.
But eventually, he said, well, we-- we haven't got unlimited money.
My mother had to sell-- she gave it to Mr.
Brooksma-- some gold-- gold watch and diamond rings and things like that.
But the money started in the end to run very low.
So my father said, well, we can't really stay with this woman because one day we won't have any more money and she will throw us out.
So through my mother-- he wasn't in contact with any underground movement, but we were.
So he said we must find a different hiding place for my father and brother.
How were you able to communicate with your father?
Well, we went occasionally-- perhaps once a month we went to visit.
We had to go on the train.
And we had our false identification papers.
And it was very scary.
But-- and sometimes we had even in trains and everybody had to show the papers.
But we spoke Dutch and it-- you know, it was-- touch what everything went all right.
But it was very dangerous.
We usually stayed the weekend.
And it was, of course, wonderful, that-- when we were together.
And my father and brother both started to paint.
And they both wrote poetry.
And that was the amazing thing.
As I said, my father was a very keen businessman and he had never time for arts, things like that.
And he had a terrific talent.
He made-- painted wonderful paintings.
All just out of his memory.
He made a nice portrait of my mother.
I've got a photo.
I'll show it to you later.
And my brother too, he made the most beautiful paintings.
Usually sad.
One picture is, for instance-- it's very somber.
And there's a woman lying in kind of an niche who had died.
And he's sitting in front of a desk, like a work clothes, crying because his mother had died.
And always very, you know, depressed pictures.
And his poetry as well.
A lot with here-- he's a bird who is free, but he's got shot down and he's dying.
And always things like that.
A lot with death.
And-- but me and he, we-- you know, we were ever so happy together.
And we cuddled.
I crept into his bed, and, you know, we had-- it was wonderful to be together.
And my father taught us bridge.
So we-- for so that when we are four, we can play bridge together.
So you know, we really enjoyed that.
And we played chess.
And it was wonderful.
I mean, over the years, you know, it was always two, three days.
So it was quite a-- quite a time we were together.
And we always looking forward to this day.
But in the meantime, we had to leave Mrs.
Klompe as well.
So we had gone to a different hiding place.
Mrs.
Klompe-- Mrs.
Klompe got scared.
She said, you know, I just-- I'm so nervous each day when I come home from school if you would still be there.
If they hadn't come to take you away.
And the nights, I can't sleep anymore.
And so we got a different hiding place with a family Reitsma.
It was-- she was an artist, and it was an older couple.
And they had their son still living at home.
And we were there only a short period when my father said they had to leave as well.
And my mother knew somebody that was not through this Mr.
Brooksma.
Because when we left Mrs.
Klompe, we lost touch with Mr.
Brooksma.
He didn't visit us at the next place.
And my mother knew somebody who said she knew somebody as well, and it was a nurse who was going to provide a hiding place for my father.
My father and brother had to escape from their hiding place because this woman wouldn't let them go, because it was a wonderful income for her.
So she wanted more money.
She still wanted them, really, to stay.
So my brother dyed his hair blonde.
He bleached it.
It became rather red.
So he was a bit embarrassed about it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that he wouldn't, you know, look so-- and in the night-- so kind of the first train, 6 o'clock in the morning, they had to take the train to Amsterdam.
That was the first time they had been going out for-- after two years.
So it was, of course, terrible nerve-wracking and scary.
And they would be met at the station by this nurse who would take them to their new hiding place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They would walk there, and they got there, and everything was all right.
They let-- but later, my father said he was suspicious because this nurse let herself in with her own key in this house.
He thought that was odd.
But you know, never thinking about anything else besides this.
And so they were there.
And the first week, we went to visit there them.
On a Saturday or Sunday, we went.
We didn't stay there.
We just went to see them.
We walked there.
And just to see how they were, and everything was fine.
And in the evening, we walked home to this Mrs.-- to the Reitsma family.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two days later we were sitting at breakfast.
There, we didn't have a hiding place-- in the hiding place.
And there was a-- that was my-- my 15th birthday, actually.
And the son of the family, [?
Floris, ?] he had left me a little parcel for my birthday.
And we were sitting down.
And there was a loud knock on the door.
And you know, nobody would come usually so early at 8 o'clock.
So we didn't really know what to do.
We were kind of frozen.
And [?
Floris, ?] he opened the window and he jumped out onto a roof and run away.
Because he was a young student who should have reported for work in Germany.
So he was sort of hiding in his own home as well.
And he run away.
And the father went down and opened the door, and there were the-- the SS with-- with Dutch police and German police.
And they run up the stairs and just looked at the table.
There was this old Mrs.
Reitsma and us two.
So she said, we come to get you.
You arrested.
And my mother said, no.
We are just visiting here.
She said, come on.
We know exactly who you are.
And no-- no stories.
And so they got us down.
And Mother said, but we-- we-- we are-- we have nothing to do with-- with-- we are not Jewish.
And-- and he said, look, stop this story.
We know exactly.
And my mother said, my-- my daughter is not Jewish.
She's a child of-- I was unfaithful to my husband.
And she is-- she's-- she's not Jewish.
And he-- he just shut her up.
Gave her a push.
And-- and we walked to the-- and the Reitsmas had to come as well.
And we went to the gestapo headquarters.
It was in a school.
And there, we had to wait in a little room, and there were lots of other people.
And of course, we were petrified.
We knew this is-- this is our end, really.
We knew as well that if you were deported and you had been in hiding, you got an S on your papers, which means [GERMAN] transport.
So punishment.
So those people were already bad-- worse treated than anybody else.
So we were just whispering.
How could it be that they got us?
And you know, we couldn't understand.
And then we called in for interrogation.
And they told us that we'd better tell everything, who helped us with the papers, and who found the hiding place, and who provided us with the identification papers.
And then they said that they had my father and brother as well.
So first, we thought they were just bluffing.
And-- but then we heard their voices.
And they were interrogating them as well.
And they told them, of course, as well that they had us.
And at first, we-- they didn't want to believe that we didn't know anything.
And we really didn't know anything.
Because luckily, the underground movement had worked that way, that you didn't know an-- a contact.
So they just started to beat us up because they wanted to have information.
But eventually, they realized.
And they threatened they would kill my brother, they said to me.
And to my father, that they would kill my mother, and things like that.
But in the end, they realized that we really didn't know anything.
And then it turned out that this nurse was a double agent and-- who had brought them to a place which she knew very well.
They were really agents with this hiding place.
And when we had gone to visit, they followed us so that they knew where we were.
So then my father-- of course, we-- there was no way-- no.
My father tried to bribe them.
He-- you know, he said, if we pay you, would you let at least the children go, or things like that.
And they said no.
But he made a deal with them.
He said, if he would give them money or jewelry or something, the Reitsmas would-- could go free.
So my father said, yes, that's-- that's fine.
And we had-- in-- we had a powder box, a talcum powder box.
And-- and you could open it to refill it.
And in that box, we had hidden diamonds and jewelry.
That was where we lived from, that was hidden there.
And that was at the Reitsmas.
So an SS man and my mother went back to the Reitsmas and fetch this powder box.
And the Germans took it and opened it and pocket it, the jewelry, of course.
And let the Reitsmas go free.
I mean, they needn't have done that.
They could have-- but at least there, they kept their word.
So at least they were saved.
I don't know the name anymore.
But after the war, there was a court case when we returned, because my mother-- at the time, of course, we didn't know the name.
And that-- I don't know exactly how it was.
But they did find her.
And there was-- my mother went to court.
But she had helped-- because she had-- as a double agent, you have to do-- to get into the underground movement, you had to go-- do good things.
And they had a few witnesses who said that she saved their life.
And so she was-- she got off.
And my mother-- till now she says that I didn't scratch her eyes out and just let her go free.
But that was still shortly after the war, and we were still in shock, really.
So yeah, she got free.
And so that evening, we were sent to the local prison in Amsterdam.
With a prison-- in a prison wagon.
And you know, I mean, it was just horrendous.
You know, suddenly from still being free to be a prisoner.
And it was a big room.
Not a cell.
With-- with lots and lots of people.
And that was the first bunk beds we got acquainted with.
And a bucket in a corner.
And horrid food and practically no washing facilities, because it was overcrowded, this prison.
And in the night, there was a woman who had an asthma attack.
And there was no medicine.
And I remember that-- you know, I still can hear it, how she-- we all thought she was dying.
You know, she couldn't breathe.
So the whole night, you know.
And nobody tried to get help from outside and nobody opened the door.
It was a terrible night.
And then we could send word to the Reitsmas and ask if they could get us some clothes, because we had nothing.
We had just what we-- and they did have-- send a little suitcase with a few things.
But my father and brother couldn't get anything, of course, because those spies where they were staying, of course, they couldn't get any clothes to them.
So they had nothing.
And--
Not in the same-- there was a woman hall and a man hall.
But it was still the same prison.
It was the ordinary prison which is still now in [DUTCH], which is a local prison, you know.
And--
Well, mainly people who were caught and people were hiding, political prisoners.
No criminals.
It was all people like that, who were going to be transported next-- in-- in-- I think next day or the day after we went to Westerbork.
By train with armed guards.
But an ordinary train.
My father again tried to bribe the guard to let us escape.
I mean, he didn't have anything to bribe.
But he said, I'll get you money if you-- I don't know what he imagined.
But anyway, there was no way.
He just-- and again I noticed that my father was really quite desperate that the whole family was caught.
And there he was completely powerless to do anything for us.
You know, I think that-- that was the thing which killed him more than the lack on food or anything, that he had to give up his family for-- you know, couldn't protect us.
So we got to Westerbork.
And that was relatively good.
It was still-- the families, was family camps.
And you didn't-- I mean, you didn't have-- have to work.
There was no forced labor.
And they had courses and all kinds of things organized.
Because some people stayed a long time there.
Some people were there many years.
And there was as well-- it was not only Jews.
There were as well the-- the Christians who had helped hide Jews, and other underground workers.
And as well the gypsies.
There were a whole part of the camp which were hundreds of gypsies.
I don't really remember them.
I know-- I only know that they were as well in the camp.
And we heard then after one or two days that the gypsies were going to be sent to Auschwitz in a few days.
And there were eight wagons full of gypsies were going to then.
And the whole transport was 12 or-- wagons.
So they were going to fill it up with Jews, the remain transport.
And as we were the last one to arrive and had no connections yet and we hadn't established ourselves, we were the ones who were going to be sent with, of course, other people.
So we never stayed much in Westerbork.
I don't know much what really happened there.
But we met a-- a man called Mr.
Hirsch who we had known in Amsterdam.
And he was the owner of a big department store.
And he had been in Westerbork for a long time.
And he gave-- from his clothes he gave for my father and brother shirts and trousers and whatever he could spare.
He gave that.
And we had, of course, a little bit of clothes.
Mrs.
Reitsma had sent a coat.
So it was May, it was warm.
And a hat.
And-- and-- so we had enough to go.
And each transport when they went east had to give food along.
They said for the people on transport.
So there was one or two wagons full of food.
And this is-- later which we realized then, every transport coming to Auschwitz had a lot of provisions, which of course wasn't used on the transport.
But it was, so to say, sent to Poland, and then the Germans used that.
No, I don't.
I don't know.
I think a German camp leader.
But it was run, really, by the Jews.
That's-- they run it camp, but the highest was where the Germans.
I think probably Dutch Jews.
But I don't really know.
I mean, we were too short there.
And you know, didn't really have any information about that.
And later, you know, I never-- I wasn't interested in the-- in the past, what I didn't-- what I didn't know.
I didn't go into that.
The only thing I know, that the people of the [DUTCH], they-- you know, the people who registered in the beginning, all that.
That was a big office.
And they were protected till the very end.
And so by that time, there were already-- because that was already late in the war-- there-- there were some people-- of course, there was no need and there were no Jews anymore in freedom in '44.
So the people of the [DUTCH] were in Westerbork, but they were pretty safe.
They were not sentenced-- they were perhaps as well in the organizations.
They were not going to send each other to the concentration camp.
A lot of those people, there were court cases against them.
So I think, you know, somebody was doing it.
So I don't think they really betrayed.
They didn't know what was going to happen.
But a lot of them got prison sentences later, those who survived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So after a few days, they told us we were going to be sent on transport with the-- with the gypsies.
So in the morning, our names were called up, and we had to go forward with whatever we had.
And to the railway line.
And there stood those-- they seemed huge to me at first, those cattle tracks with two sliding metal doors, and on top just a very narrow little window, an opening.
And all iron metal, you know, the whole tracks.
And so we were herded in there.
Still together, men and women together.
But much too many, of course.
It was so cramped that not everybody could sit down.
We had to take turns in sitting or standing and lying.
And it was pretty dark once the doors were closed.
There was, again, just one bucket in a corner for toilet facilities.
There was no-- nothing in it.
Nothing at all.
No mattress.
No table.
Nothing.
So there we sat.
And then slowly the train set into motion.
And that was really the last of our relative freedom which we have had then.
Nobody said where we were going.
But I mean, from Holland, you know you were going east.
And that was a journey took many, many days.
We were going east, and the-- the German troops were going west.
Because the invasion was-- they were already expecting, you know.
So there was a lot of troop transport.
So very often they put our train on the side line, and we had to wait for hours till there was room again to put us into it.
And it was unbearably hot and smelly and practically no air.
It was terrible.
Because if this train stands still and there was no air, and it was hot summer day.
It was awful.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Occasionally, they opened the door to change the buckets.
And once per day, you got kind of bread thrown in like wild beasts.
And once they came in and we had to give all our valuables, watches, rings, everything we had to give.
They collected that.
Some people threw through the window, threw notes out telling who they were and where they are and what they-- what's-- that they going to be transported.
Some people even threw rings out with it.
Of course, don't know what happened to it.
We don't know if it was just in the country or near the village.
We didn't see anything.
And like this we traveled for many days.
And my father gave us instructions how to behave in camp.
Never sit on a toilet because you might get germs.
And always wash your hands as much as possible because-- so that you don't get ill.
And-- and all kind of little things like that.
Well, those are the things I remembered most, you know?
And of course, he said that it can't-- it gave us hope.
It won't be long anymore, and we'll get together again.
And just be strong.
And we'll make the most of it.
And you know, things like that.
And then after a few days, this train stopped and we heard shouting.
And noise.
And they opened the doors, and the Germans with their shouting, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And we saw them standing with machine guns towards-- pointing towards the doors.
And Alsatian dogs on leash-- [DUTCH].
